words: This interview with Glen and Agnes Gilder was recorded at their home near Spring
Valley on July 29, 1975. Interviewer Sam Schrager, Moscow, Idaho.
GLEN GILDER: We were around there, you couldn't help but know him. Laird and
Humiston.
SAM SCHRAGER: Do you remember Laird as being sort of an aristocratic kind of a
man?

speaker: GG
words: Oh, I think he walked with his shoulders back, alright, but I've seen

speaker: GG
words: men with less that had more swelled head. (Chuckles) He wasn't over

speaker: GG
words: bearing or anything. He knew you. No matter where he met you. No mat

speaker: GG
words: ter how you were dressed. Then a little bit later they had another one,

speaker: GG
words: Irwin. Same way. And O'Connell and some of them birds, they

speaker: GG
words: wanted you to know that they were boss. Big shot. A lot of them. There's

speaker: GG
words: been a string of them, since those two or three. Laird and Irwin. Irwin

speaker: GG
words: didn't stay too long. And O'Connell came and he stayed about ten or

speaker: GG
words: twelve years. Then there's been a string of 'em since, I don t know who

speaker: GG
words: they are. I liked Irwin the best of all of 'em.

speaker: SS
words: What was he like? What made him—?

speaker: GG
words: Friendly. Just a common old lumberjack, in that big position. But it

speaker: GG
words: didn't go to his head. It's not uncommon and it never has been for a man

speaker: GG
words: to get up with a little position to get swelled up about it. Some of 'em

speaker: GG
words: were and some of 'em weren't.

speaker: SS
words: I've heard that a number of those people on Nob Hill were not all as well

speaker: SS
words: off, maybe, as they would want people to think.

speaker: GG
words: There was Laird and Humiston and Sagerson. And then around on the side

speaker: GG
words: was Erick Matson and George Anderson. And Matson was assistant superin

speaker: GG
words: tendent, and George Anderson was the banker and Howard Hanson lived up

speaker: GG
words: there, he worked in the bank. Oh, it was kinda supposed to be upper crust,

speaker: GG
words: nice fellows up there, and pretty good people. I got awful well acquainted with them,

speaker: GG
words: because we had a dairy over there and I

speaker: GG
words: called at them houses day after day after day with their milk. I got to

speaker: GG
words: know 'em real well.

speaker: SS
words: I wondered if they'd have much to do with the ordinary people.

speaker: GG
words: They kinda separated, yeah, they did. They seldom went to the public

speaker: GG
words: dances. the gym. Once in a while there'd be some of em in there, but

speaker: GG
words: not too many. They kinda had their own clique and they stayed in that

speaker: GG
words: clique. You're talking about Nob Hill.

speaker: GG
words: AGNES GILDER: Snob Hill?

speaker: SS
words: Snob Hill! (Chuckles) Is that what they used to call it?

speaker: GG
words: Oh, they called it everything!! (Laughter) Well, I couldn't see anything

speaker: GG
words: different the people lived on that hill and the people that lived on

speaker: GG
words: the other one across the road.

speaker: AG
words: Oh, a few of 'em mixed, not many.

speaker: GG
words: That Shelton Landers outfit, they lived right back of the church and

speaker: GG
words: they were right in between.

speaker: AG
words: You better not tell that one, because—

speaker: SS
words: Oh, that's okay, sounds good.

speaker: AG
words: No, no, you don't need to tell that. He is still around isn't he?

speaker: GG
words: Shelt? No he's dead. But anyway, I think he was the biggest snob in the

speaker: GG
words: whole damn town, ismy opinion of him!

speaker: SS
words: What was he like?

speaker: GG
words: He was a master mechanic. And he got out of school, and just because he

speaker: GG
words: was Shelton Landers, why, Laird put him right in (as) top of the machine

speaker: GG
words: shop.

speaker: AG
words: Was he some relation to Laird?

speaker: GG
words: I don't think so.

speaker: SS
words: What was it? Just his manner? jUst his bearing?

speaker: GG
words: I think so. I was sent into the shop over there with a job to be done

speaker: GG
words: at the forge, and Ole was the head blacksmith and he's a very

speaker: GG
words: close friend of mine. So Shelt come walking in; I was standing there

speaker: GG
words: by the forge and Ole was working and we were talking and Shelt told me

speaker: GG
words: that the only people allowed in here were to be working, and turned around

speaker: GG
words: and walked away. I had been sent in there by one of the other

speaker: GG
words: bosses, so the thing for me to do was to stay there and I did. Pretty

speaker: GG
words: soon he come back. "I told you, that the people that are in here are

speaker: GG
words: supposed to be working." "I don't know whether I'm working or not, but

speaker: GG
words: Brock sent me over here with a job to be done, and I expect Id pro

speaker: GG
words: bably get it done." And he turned around and walked away again. That

speaker: GG
words: was the last I seen of him, but the only thought he had, he didn't give

speaker: GG
words: a damn whether I was there or not, he wanted to throw his voice. And I

speaker: GG
words: let him throw it. (Chuckles) But, we were close to the same age, we

speaker: GG
words: knew each other we were going to school, and before we were mar

speaker: GG
words: ried, around at dances and things. But we never cliqued.

speaker: SS
words: So, it really went to his head, when he got a little power..

speaker: GG
words: Uh-huh. Well, when he went to Lewiston from over there, he went down to

speaker: GG
words: run the machine shop in Lewiston, and this Johnny Johnson that owned

speaker: GG
words: this place up here where where Milton Baird is now, or has, was under

speaker: GG
words: Shelt. And pretty soon he came up the line and went over Shelt, and I

speaker: GG
words: knew it and one day I asked Johnny, "How do you get along with Shelt,

speaker: GG
words: Johnny?" "Real good." He said, "There's nobody could get along with the

speaker: GG
words: S.O.B.." (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: He probably didn't have any friends.

speaker: GG
words: Oh, I think he had a lot of friends, that'd scratch his elbow just for

speaker: GG
words: the position he held, and assumed that he held. I think there was a lot

speaker: GG
words: of that going on. More of that went on in Potlatch than anyplace I ever

speaker: SS
words: What were you going to say?

speaker: GG
words: Well, think I've said too much already! (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: No. Got any matches?

speaker: GG
words: Yeah.

speaker: AG
words: Why don't you put that thing off?

speaker: GG
words: Is that damn thing on? (Laughter)

speaker: SS
words: Ready?

speaker: GG
words: I think so. Potlatch was just a crosscut of all these little industrial

speaker: GG
words: towns at that time. They had to have— they thought they had to have a

speaker: GG
words: separation in society. And that's the way it is. It's the laboring

speaker: GG
words: man and the office on up. But you know, I had a funny way of judging

speaker: GG
words: that proposition. I was selling milk in there and I sold a lot on Nob

speaker: GG
words: Hill and on all the rest of the hills. Those fellows in the higher

speaker: GG
words: brackets were always employed. They always had money and they were good

speaker: GG
words: pay. Well, over on the other hill, they weren't always employed, they'd

speaker: GG
words: be laid off periodically for one reason and another, and a week after

speaker: GG
words: they were laid off they were broke. But even people up on Nob Hill

speaker: GG
words: they wouldn't have to be laid off, they would stall me off for anything

speaker: GG
words: from a few days to a month on that milk bill, that was only from three to

speaker: GG
words: six dollars a month.

speaker: AG
words: Twenty-five cents a quart, wasn't it?

speaker: GG
words: No, twelve and a half.

speaker: AG
words: Eight cents a quart.

speaker: SS
words: Huh. That wouldn't add up to too much.

speaker: GG
words: No. No. You take a quart or two — like Eric Matson had quite a family,

speaker: GG
words: I think he took three quarts. But it would amaze you, the people up

speaker: GG
words: there in that higher bracket, for no reason at all you could see, they

speaker: GG
words: have to stall you off for ten, fifteen, twenty days to make the payment.

speaker: GG
words: Well, I had people over on the other hill, the common laboring class,

speaker: GG
words: and I know three or four of them that were laid off clear through that

speaker: GG
words: Depression and never missed a doggone payment. So I figured the best

speaker: GG
words: men were over on that hill, in my estimation. The most capable men were

speaker: GG
words: on the other hill. Carl Hague never missed a payment— and how many

speaker: GG
words: children did they have? Eight?

speaker: AG
words: I don't remember.

speaker: GG
words: There quite a bunch of 'em.

speaker: SS
words: These Nob Hill people still had jobs with Potlatch during that time?

speaker: GG
words: The Nob Hill bunch were yes. They were steady employed because they

speaker: GG
words: were railroad— Gamble and his crew, Marshing and Elsie, and all those

speaker: GG
words: people they were employed every doggone day, while the mill crew— see

speaker: GG
words: the mill shut down and most of 'em were just in a desperate fix in a

speaker: GG
words: pnsin bunch, few days. But there was quite a few of 'em,that weren't ever hurt too

speaker: GG
words: bad.

speaker: SS
words: Doesn't seem like a lot of those people, from what you're saying, really

speaker: SS
words: got ahead very much by the time they're done with what they had to spend

speaker: SS
words: their wages on. Some of them just got by for years.

speaker: SS
words: Well, most of 'em, that's true. But this Ole Ngaard, he told me this

speaker: SS
words: story several times— He came over from the old country and he landed

speaker: SS
words: in Potlatch practically broke. I think he had less than five dollars.

speaker: SS
words: So he worked a week or two and made a draw and he bought an old second

speaker: SS
words: hand cookstove and rented a house and enough dishes and stuff to— so

speaker: SS
words: he could cook his own meals and bached. And he waited then until he

speaker: SS
words: got another payday and he bought some more blankets that he needed and

speaker: SS
words: some more dishes that he needed and got his bills all squared away, but

speaker: SS
words: he didn't have a bed to sleep on. He didn't have a table nor a chair in

speaker: SS
words: He had some bedding but he slept on the floor. And he

speaker: SS
words: said, "I just saved every cent I could, and after a while," he said, "I

speaker: SS
words: had enough money saved up to send to the old country for my wife and

speaker: SS
words: kids." And, he says, "They came, and I had to get a few more dishes

speaker: SS
words: and a little more bedding," he said, "but, we were two months before we

speaker: SS
words: had money enough to get tables and chairs and bedsteads!" They slept

speaker: SS
words: on the floor- andiate on boxes, just bached in that company house. And

speaker: SS
words: when Ole wound up he was wealthy. He owned one of the nicest places

speaker: SS
words: between Palouse and Potlatch. Just a hell of a good man. He didn't

speaker: SS
words: have anything else, just what he saved out of his wages until he bought

speaker: SS
words: the place. And then after he got it paid for, then he had a very good

speaker: SS
words: income from his place. I think he told me, the last time I talked to

speaker: SS
words: him, he told me it took him twenty-eight years to pay for that place.

speaker: SS
words: "But," he says, by gosh, all paid for.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think— did he have a better job?

speaker: GG
words: He was a blacksmith. A forge blacksmith. And he is absolutely an art

speaker: GG
words: ist. Anything you could throw in front of him, if there was a broken

speaker: GG
words: iron in that mill, he could fix it. And that's a fact. They had artists

speaker: GG
words: in those days— they had a man that used to fix them belts, the belts

speaker: GG
words: were all leather, and they were twelve feet wide down to an inch and a

speaker: GG
words: half. Lee Larimore was a belt man and if they'd break a belt he had

speaker: GG
words: another one he'd slip on and then he had a bunch of adjustable wheels

speaker: GG
words: that he'd put just the width that he wanted 'em, for most belts, but not

speaker: GG
words: all of 'em. And then he'd get in there with a plane and he'd plane that

speaker: GG
words: leather down and he d insert a patch of that leather there with glue

speaker: GG
words: and this and that and you couldn't hardly tell where it was patched.

speaker: GG
words: He was just that good. You know that big belt that run that whole sawmill,

speaker: GG
words: eight or ten or twelve feet wide, I don't remember, they were wide

speaker: GG
words: and they broke that one day and he didn't have any place to splice it

speaker: GG
words: excepting right on the wheel. He made a couple of clamps and put on

speaker: GG
words: there and bolted 'em together, pulled that belt back to where he wanted

speaker: GG
words: it, and then he started in and he spliced that belt, and so help me,

speaker: GG
words: you couldn't hardly tell where he'd been working on it! Just that smooth.

speaker: GG
words: He'd leave one piece of leather a little bit thick, next one under it

speaker: GG
words: he'd thin it down just a certain amount, and then he'd makeAkind of a

speaker: GG
words: wedge. There was three or four plies and no two plies had the joint in

speaker: GG
words: the same place; they'd be about that far apart. (Indicates width with

speaker: GG
words: hands). And he'd spliced that belt one evening when Iwas on shift, on

speaker: GG
words: the afternoon shift, and I spent half of my shift watching him.

speaker: SS
words: Did guys like him and the blacksmiths, who were really highly skilled

speaker: SS
words: in their field, did they get much better pay than the others?

speaker: GG
words: I think so. I think they did. But it'd just be peanuts to what they

speaker: GG
words: would get today. And those skills don't exist. You couldn't find a

speaker: GG
words: man in the State of Idaho could splice a belt, I'll bet you!! I don't

speaker: GG
words: think you could find a man in the State of Idaho could make a tool

speaker: GG
words: as quick and it would stand up as good as old Ole could. Ed Compton,

speaker: GG
words: and Stover Douglas and Mike Murphy over there at Millwrights

speaker: GG
words: They had a big job on their hands, that's a big mill,but they kept her

speaker: GG
words: running. If they had to work all night, they'd work, but they got her.

speaker: GG
words: Malcolm Greer was one of the crew. Ed Compton, Stover Douglas, Mike

speaker: GG
words: Murphy, Guy Puckett— if there's any more've forgotten. By darn if

speaker: GG
words: she broke down, they might miss their supper and breakfast, but they'd

speaker: GG
words: get her together! They grew up in sawmills and they grew up under

speaker: GG
words: men that had more adverse conditions in a sawmill than they had, and

speaker: GG
words: they really knew their business. They came West with the company and

speaker: GG
words: died right there on the job, you know, in town anyway.

speaker: SS
words: Were they a set crew? The maintenance crew? And that was their job.

speaker: GG
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Was most of that work usually done at night?

speaker: GG
words: Yes. Well, if they had to shut a machine down and there was room to

speaker: GG
words: work around it, then they could do it and the rest of the mill running,

speaker: GG
words: but if a machine couldn't be worked on while the mill was running, if it

speaker: GG
words: wasn't something that shut the saws and the edger and so forth down,why

speaker: GG
words: they just keep right on going and then when the whistle blew these guys'd

speaker: GG
words: pitch into it. You know you hear men whine and grumble about their jobs

speaker: GG
words: now being such a headache and such a hard job and all this and that.

speaker: GG
words: I've known them men to be called out at midnight; something'd break and

speaker: GG
words: it might be thirty below zero. They didn't kick, they come down there

speaker: GG
words: just as good natured as if they . going to a party; go to work, just

speaker: GG
words: fine. You never hear one of 'em kick.

speaker: SS
words: I sort of wonder about these hours and the wages in those days— Art

speaker: SS
words: Sundberg was telling me it was a ten hour day—

speaker: GG
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: No overtime.

speaker: GG
words: No.

speaker: SS
words: And you could expect on a lot of jobs, fairly often, to work an extra

speaker: SS
words: two and a half hours in the mill.

speaker: GG
words: That's right. For some reason, always needed, and you were expected to

speaker: GG
words: do it. No excuse. Take us guys that were on shift, and somebody on

speaker: GG
words: one side or the other of us would be sick or couldn't come to work, or

speaker: GG
words: didn't come to work, if you were on the shift and he was next, no quest

speaker: GG
words: ions asked, you stay right there the next eight hours. Sixteen hours

speaker: GG
words: straight through. And you were lucky your wife or somebody brought

speaker: GG
words: your lunch, that was fine, but if they didn't, that was fine, too. No

speaker: GG
words: body give a damn. That seems unbelievable, but it's a fact.

speaker: SS
words: You guys didn't think twice about it yourself. Did you feel that it

speaker: SS
words: was pretty tough then, or did it just seem the way things ought to be

speaker: SS
words: done.

speaker: GG
words: No. It was just something — what had to be done, and it just fell your

speaker: GG
words: luck to do it, so, do it and keep quiet about it. Just got paid at the

speaker: GG
words: same rate per hour for sixteen hours, as if you had just put in eight

speaker: GG
words: hours. No difference. You just got sixteen hours. Oh, we liked that

speaker: GG
words: same as they like overtime now. In a way, we liked it financially, but

speaker: GG
words: it was— lots of them jobs were darn hard work, and you were pretty damn

speaker: GG
words: tired when night come, after sixteen hours of it.

speaker: SS
words: What were you doing in the mill when you started working there?

speaker: GG
words: Firing the boilers.

speaker: SS
words: When was it?

speaker: GG
words: 1928. Started the mill. I fired for two or three years, and then got

speaker: GG
words: in a fight and got promoted.

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, you told me about that. (Chuckles) That was a good story.

speaker: SS
words: Glen what was the story you told me about the doctor telling you that

speaker: SS
words: you were going to stay there? What was that?

speaker: GG
words: Oh, I told him I— he asked me what I was doing and I told him I worked

speaker: GG
words: in the mill. He figure if you ever started to work in the mill

speaker: GG
words: you'd never leave it. And most of 'em didn't, either.

speaker: SS
words: What do you think that caused so much brownnosing in a place like Pot

speaker: SS
words: latch?

speaker: GG
words: Well, actually it wasn't any worse at Potlatch than it was any place

speaker: GG
words: else. There's a class of people even today that'll do that. Try to pro

speaker: GG
words: mote themselves, if they have to demote somebody else, that's all right.

speaker: GG
words: But I heard a preacher say one time that that's a sin and I wouldn't be

speaker: GG
words: surprised that maybe it is. I don't know. You take a hundred men in a

speaker: GG
words: crew and you're just going to find it then or now or fifty years before

speaker: GG
words: or fifty years after. They never gain anything by it, never. Oh, boys

speaker: GG
words: like Art can tell you that sawmill story better than I could, because

speaker: GG
words: I didn't stay there— ten years— 'cause my wings weren't clipped. Art

speaker: GG
words: spent his life there, and I don't blame him. Bryberrys ther— and

speaker: GG
words: Comptons other and Tom Donohue's another and just go on and on.

speaker: GG
words: They spent their life there. I don't think they did wrong.

speaker: GG
words: (End of Side A)

speaker: SS
words: I was thinking about the other day-just a cog in those wheels. There's

speaker: SS
words: so many hundreds of people involved in getting out these products. And

speaker: SS
words: other people are profiting quite a bit from it, the stockholders, the

speaker: SS
words: shareholders. The difference between that and the small farmer, who

speaker: SS
words: was the kind of guy that broke the frontier and pioneered the country—

speaker: SS
words: they seem like such a different way of life to me. Like the old way

speaker: SS
words: that made the West and then this new way which sort of dominates Amer

speaker: SS
words: ica— it seems like a real big difference when I think about it and

speaker: SS
words: the kind of way the whole business is set up. You're a farmer, you're

speaker: SS
words: just doing it for yourself; one-man operation, and if you're working

speaker: SS
words: for the mill you're part of a big corporation.

speaker: GG
words: You are a cog. Well, there is, there always has been and always will

speaker: GG
words: be people that can fit in and be a crew and under a boss and they're

speaker: GG
words: satisfied there. And it isn't any reflection on their judgement or any

speaker: GG
words: thing; it's what they like to do. Now they don't have any responsibility

speaker: GG
words: excepting just this little niche that they're in. And then you find guys

speaker: GG
words: like myself that don't like to work for somebody else, and don't like to

speaker: GG
words: be told what to do. And if I earn a dollar, I don't want to split it

speaker: GG
words: seventy to thirty, or ninety to ten. That's the reason fellows like me

speaker: GG
words: get away from it, and that's the reason that the other guys that are

speaker: GG
words: satisfied, they stay there. Now chances are good that they had a darn

speaker: GG
words: sight easier life, less worry and less bother, or less worry and less

speaker: GG
words: concern than I had, because I had to fight my way through it, moneywise,

speaker: GG
words: get the work done, get equipment to do the work with and all those things

speaker: GG
words: They didn't have that worry. They didn't want it. But I did. I wanted

speaker: GG
words: to fight my own, and that's the reason that I got away from it. Those

speaker: GG
words: guys are retired, they got problems all solved, a little savings and

speaker: GG
words: pretty good pension, and they're fine, they're just okay. And I'm just

speaker: GG
words: in the same boat, that's all. After it's all wound up, I'm just the

speaker: GG
words: same boat. I'm coasting along fine, doing alright. That's what I worked

speaker: GG
words: forwhat I had planned and I made it.

speaker: SS
words: Course the thing that I think goes along with that, which I think would

speaker: SS
words: be tough, I wonder how people would a place like Potlatch where you

speaker: SS
words: got all the brass up on Nob Hill— There's such a hierarchy, or such a

speaker: SS
words: system, you got your place and then these other guys are above you and

speaker: SS
words: then Laird sits there on top of the whole heap and you have to live with

speaker: SS
words: it. It seemed very set up in a pattern that way. Well, the guy who

speaker: SS
words: works for himself isn't

speaker: GG
words: Well, he's not in that hierarchy, but at the same time, don't think he

speaker: GG
words: don't have problems. But, those guys had a good life? and they're doing

speaker: GG
words: fine now. And, after all, what else is there?

speaker: SS
words: I was going to ask you about when you started working here, when you were

speaker: SS
words: a kid. What work did you do, when you started working out?

speaker: GG
words: Anything I could get. Generally I could get a job driving somebody's

speaker: GG
words: horses. Doing something. That come just as natural to me as breathing.

speaker: GG
words: And I generally followed that. Then when I went into the mill, I worked

speaker: GG
words: around the boilers for a few years. Fired and tended water and watched

speaker: GG
words: what was going on, and when I left there, why then I went out running a

speaker: GG
words: engine in a small mill. And I run that until steam went out of style,

speaker: GG
words: and then that was it. Then I learned to edge and finished out a few years

speaker: GG
words: farming and sawmilling— sawmill nights— or sawmill daytimes and farm

speaker: GG
words: nights.

speaker: SS
words: Farm nights?

speaker: GG
words: Yeah. And Sundays. For quite a few years I and the boys did all the

speaker: GG
words: work here on the place, kept it right up to snuff.

speaker: SS
words: With a headlight on the horseteam?

speaker: GG
words: No. No, didn't seem like we had to. We didn't need a headlight until

speaker: GG
words: we got a tractor. We'd always keep right up . Than after we got a trac

speaker: GG
words: tor— 'course we had more land by that time and more to do. I used to

speaker: GG
words: figure:come home Friday night and run til eleven or twelve o'clock with

speaker: GG
words: the tractor, then come in and sleep and go back out probably eight o'clock

speaker: GG
words: and run til eleven or twelve again. Then on Sunday—then I was late up,

speaker: GG
words: I'd figure on getting to work about eight or nine and I'd quit at six

speaker: GG
words: or someplace along there, so I wouldn't be too groggy on Monday morning.

speaker: GG
words: Made it alright. When I got through and retired Social Security sent

speaker: GG
words: me a list of every penny I'd made all my life, or it was supposed to be,

speaker: GG
words: but it was what I'd made since they'd passed the Social Security law.

speaker: GG
words: bo L at that, and then one day I was just studying around here

speaker: GG
words: and adding up the value of the place and the value of the equipment on
